content:
First, label the trapezoid so that \(BC\) is the longer base of length \(b\), \(AD\) the shorter base of length \(a\), and the equal legs \(AB=CD=\ell\).  Then

(1)  “trapezoid‐perimeter” \(a+b+2\ell=120\),  
(2)  “triangle \(ABC\)‐perimeter” \(\ell+b+d=114\),  

where \(d=AC=BD\) is the common diagonal (in an isosceles trapezoid the diagonals are equal).  

Next, put \(BC\) on the \(x\)–axis with \(B=(0,0),\;C=(b,0)\), and place \(A,D\) on the horizontal line \(y=h\) so that \(AD\parallel BC\) and the figure is symmetric about \(x=b/2\).  One finds
\[
A=\Bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2,\;h\Bigr),\quad
D=\Bigl(\tfrac{b+a}2,\;h\Bigr).
\]
By the usual formula for the incenter of a triangle as a weighted average of the vertices, the two incenters \(I_1\) of \(\triangle ABC\) and \(I_2\) of \(\triangle DBC\) both lie at the same height \(y=\tfrac{b\,h}{114}\), and their \(x\)–coordinates differ by
\[
x_{I_2}-x_{I_1}\;=\;\frac{b\,(a+d-\ell)}{114}.
\]
We are told this distance is \(16\); hence
\[
b\,(a+d-\ell)=114\cdot16=1824.
\]
Meanwhile from (1) and (2)
\[
a=120-b-2\ell,\qquad
d=114-b-\ell.
\]
A (straightforward but somewhat lengthy) algebraic elimination of \(a,d,h\) in favor of \(b,\ell\) shows
\[
\ell=25,\quad b=48,\quad a=22,\quad d=41,
\]
and the trapezoid’s height
\[
h\;=\;\sqrt{\ell^2-\Bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\Bigr)^2}
\;=\;\sqrt{25^2-13^2}
\;=\;2\sqrt{114}.
\]
Finally the area of the trapezoid is
\[
\frac{a+b}2\cdot h
\;=\;\frac{22+48}2\;\cdot\;2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;35\cdot2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;70\sqrt{114}.
\]
Thus \(m=70,\;n=114\) and
\[
100m+n \;=\;7000+114 \;=\;\boxed{7114}.
\]

message.content:
First, label the trapezoid so that \(BC\) is the longer base of length \(b\), \(AD\) the shorter base of length \(a\), and the equal legs \(AB=CD=\ell\).  Then

(1)  “trapezoid‐perimeter” \(a+b+2\ell=120\),  
(2)  “triangle \(ABC\)‐perimeter” \(\ell+b+d=114\),  

where \(d=AC=BD\) is the common diagonal (in an isosceles trapezoid the diagonals are equal).  

Next, put \(BC\) on the \(x\)–axis with \(B=(0,0),\;C=(b,0)\), and place \(A,D\) on the horizontal line \(y=h\) so that \(AD\parallel BC\) and the figure is symmetric about \(x=b/2\).  One finds
\[
A=\Bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2,\;h\Bigr),\quad
D=\Bigl(\tfrac{b+a}2,\;h\Bigr).
\]
By the usual formula for the incenter of a triangle as a weighted average of the vertices, the two incenters \(I_1\) of \(\triangle ABC\) and \(I_2\) of \(\triangle DBC\) both lie at the same height \(y=\tfrac{b\,h}{114}\), and their \(x\)–coordinates differ by
\[
x_{I_2}-x_{I_1}\;=\;\frac{b\,(a+d-\ell)}{114}.
\]
We are told this distance is \(16\); hence
\[
b\,(a+d-\ell)=114\cdot16=1824.
\]
Meanwhile from (1) and (2)
\[
a=120-b-2\ell,\qquad
d=114-b-\ell.
\]
A (straightforward but somewhat lengthy) algebraic elimination of \(a,d,h\) in favor of \(b,\ell\) shows
\[
\ell=25,\quad b=48,\quad a=22,\quad d=41,
\]
and the trapezoid’s height
\[
h\;=\;\sqrt{\ell^2-\Bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\Bigr)^2}
\;=\;\sqrt{25^2-13^2}
\;=\;2\sqrt{114}.
\]
Finally the area of the trapezoid is
\[
\frac{a+b}2\cdot h
\;=\;\frac{22+48}2\;\cdot\;2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;35\cdot2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;70\sqrt{114}.
\]
Thus \(m=70,\;n=114\) and
\[
100m+n \;=\;7000+114 \;=\;\boxed{7114}.
\]
